py_libraries.ml.preprocessing.MinMaxGlobalScaler

class py_libraries.ml.preprocessing.MinMaxGlobalScaler(feature_range=(0, 1), copy=True, clip=False, log=False, dtype=<class 'numpy.float64'>)[source]

Globally scale numeric data to a given feature range using the minimum and maximum computed over all fitted values. The fitted data can be arbitrarily nested (e.g., [[1], [2]], [1, 2, 3], or deeper structures), and transformed data can have a different shape.

The transformation is:

X_std = (X - global_min) / (global_max - global_min) X_scaled = X_std * (max - min) + min

where (min, max) is the desired feature_range.

Parameters:

  • feature_range (tuple (min, max), default=(0, 1)) – Desired range for the transformed data.

  • copy (bool, default=True) – If True, work on a copy of the input data.

  • clip (bool, default=False) – If True, clip transformed values to the given feature_range.

  • log (bool, default=False) – If True, a logarithme is apply to the values. Usefull when the values have different orders.

  • dtype (data-type, default=np.float64) – Desired floating point precision for the computations (e.g., np.float32 for lower precision).

data_min_

Global minimum of the fitted data.

Type:

float

data_max_

Global maximum of the fitted data.

Type:

float

data_range_

Global range (data_max_ - data_min_).

Type:

float

scale_
Scaling factor computed as:

(feature_range[1] - feature_range[0]) / (data_range_)

Type:

float

min_
Offset computed as:

feature_range[0] - data_min_ * scale_

Type:

float

__init__(feature_range=(0, 1), copy=True, clip=False, log=False, dtype=<class 'numpy.float64'>)[source]

Methods

__init__([feature_range, copy, clip, log, dtype])

fit(X[, y])

Compute the global minimum and maximum from the fitted data.

fit_transform(X[, y])

Fit the scaler to X, then transform X.

inverse_transform(X)

Reverse the transformation.

transform(X)

Scale the input data using the global minimum and maximum computed during fit.
